title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Spiritual Path
meta_description: Trust your inner knowing to guide your spiritual path. Stop overthinking. Your intuition is a built in compass you already have. Learn to use it clearly.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Spiritual Path
og_description: Trust your inner knowing to guide your spiritual path. Stop overthinking. Your intuition is a built in compass you already have. Learn to use it clearly.
body:
You Already Know More Than You Think You Do

Let's be real for a second. If you're reading this, you've probably spent years trying to figure out your spiritual path by reading books, watching YouTube videos, and collecting more tools than you could ever use. You've got crystals, oracle decks, meditation apps, and a bookshelf that's starting to look like a New Age bookstore. And yet, when it comes to making the actual decisions about your spiritual path, you still feel like you're guessing.

I get it. I've been there. The thing is, you don't need more information. You need to trust what you already know.

Your intuition isn't some mystical gift reserved for gurus and psychics. It's a biological system you were born with - a built-in guidance mechanism that's been working for you your whole life. The problem isn't that you don't have intuition. The problem is that you've been taught to ignore it, override it, or explain it away. Right?!

So let's talk about how to actually use your intuition when making decisions about your spiritual path. Not in theory. Not in some abstract, "follow your bliss" kind of way. I mean practically, tangibly, in the real world where you have bills to pay and a job to show up to and relationships to work through.

"Your intuition isn't a voice from the heavens. It's a whisper from your own body, and it's been trying to talk to you your whole life. The only question is whether you're ready to listen."

Why Your Intuition Is the Only Tool You Actually Need

Here's the thing about spiritual paths: there are about a million of them. You've got Buddhism, Christianity, paganism, shamanism, energy work, channeling, mediumship, plant medicine, breathwork, yoga, tantra, and about fifty different flavors of "I made up my own thing and it works for me."

Every single one of them has people who swear it's the one true path. Every single one has teachers who will tell you that their way is the right way. And every single one has a community that will welcome you if you follow their rules.

But here's what nobody tells you: your spiritual path is not about finding the right system. It's about finding the system that works for you. And the only way to know what works for you is to check in with your body's response to it.

Your intuition is the filter. It's the thing that tells you whether a teaching lands in your bones or just bounces off your brain. It's the thing that tells you whether a practice opens you up or shuts you down. It's the thing that tells you whether a teacher is worth your time or just someone who's good at sounding wise.

And the reason you need to learn to use your intuition for this is simple: nobody else can do it for you. No guru, no book, no podcast, no course. They can give you information, but they can't give you wisdom. Wisdom comes from the inside. It comes from that quiet knowing that doesn't need to be explained or justified.

The Problem With Making Spiritual Decisions From Your Head

Most people make spiritual decisions the same way they make every other decision: they think about it. They make pros and cons lists. They research. They compare. They analyze. They ask other people what they think.

And then they end up more confused than when they started.

That's because your mind is not designed to make spiritual decisions. Your mind is designed to keep you safe, to solve practical problems, and to work through the physical world. When you try to use it to work through the spiritual world, it gets overwhelmed. It doesn't have the right tools. It's like trying to use a hammer to fix a computer - you can do it, but you're going to break something.

Your intuition, on the other hand, is designed for exactly this kind of thing. It operates outside of logic. It doesn't need to understand why something feels right - it just knows. It doesn't need to explain itself. It doesn't need to justify its choices. It simply communicates through sensation, through feeling, through that quiet inner knowing that doesn't have words.

When you try to make spiritual decisions from your head, you end up in a loop. You read about shamanism, and it sounds interesting. Then you read about Buddhism, and that also sounds interesting. Then you read about Christianity, and you remember some good things about that too. You can't decide, so you read more. And more. And more. And you never actually commit to anything.

Sound familiar? Does that land?

The way out of this loop is to stop thinking and start feeling. To stop gathering information and start checking in with your body. To stop asking "which path is right?" and start asking "which path feels right for me?"

How Your Body Talks to You About Spiritual Truth

Your body has a wisdom that your mind can't touch. It knows things before you do. It responds to truth and falsehood, to resonance and dissonance, to alignment and misalignment - all without needing a single thought.

Here's what I want you to try. Next time you're considering a spiritual practice, a teacher, a book, or a path, don't think about it. Don't analyze it. Instead, bring your attention to your body and notice what happens.

Does your chest feel open or tight? Does your stomach feel relaxed or knotted? Do your shoulders drop or do they creep up toward your ears? Does your breathing get deeper or shallower? Do you feel a sense of expansion or contraction?

These are not random sensations. They are your intuition speaking to you in the only language it has - the language of the body. And if you learn to listen to them, they will tell you everything you need to know.

Openness, relaxation, expansion, deeper breathing - these are signs of resonance. They mean that what you're considering is aligned with your truth. It's a yes from your body.

Tightness, tension, contraction, shallow breathing - these are signs of dissonance. They mean that something is off. It might not be wrong in any objective sense, but it's wrong for you. It's a no from your body.

The tricky part is that your mind will try to override these signals. It will tell you that you're being silly, that you're making things up, that you need more evidence, that you should give it more time, that you don't have enough information to decide. Don't listen to it. Your body knows. It has always known. You just haven't been paying attention.

Three Practical Steps to Make Intuitive Decisions About Your Path

Okay, so let's get practical. Here's how you actually do this. Not in theory, but in real life, starting today.

Step 1: Create Space for the Signal

Your intuition can't talk to you if you're constantly filling your head with noise. You need to create space for the signal to come through. This means turning off the podcasts, closing the books, putting down your phone, and just sitting with yourself for a few minutes.

I'm not talking about a full meditation practice. I'm talking about five minutes of quiet, with your hand on your heart, asking yourself a simple question: "What do I need to know right now?"

Don't expect an answer in words. Expect a feeling. Expect a sensation. Expect a shift in your energy. The answer will come in whatever form your intuition chooses to speak to you. It might be a word, an image, a memory, a physical sensation, or just a knowing that doesn't have any content at all.

The key is to not force it. You're not trying to make something happen. You're creating conditions for something to happen naturally. Like opening a window and letting the breeze come in - you don't have to push the air, you just have to open the window.

Step 2: Test the Decision in Your Body

Once you've created some space, bring the decision you're facing into your awareness. Let's say you're trying to decide whether to commit to a particular spiritual practice or tradition. Imagine yourself fully committed to it. See yourself doing the practices, attending the gatherings, being part of the community. Feel what it would be like to say yes to this path.

Now check in with your body. What do you notice? Does it feel like a relief? Does it feel like a burden? Does it feel like coming home or like putting on someone else's clothes?

Then imagine yourself walking away from it. Imagine saying no, choosing something else, or choosing nothing at all. Check in with your body again. What do you notice now? Does it feel like freedom or loss? Does it feel like peace or regret?

Your body's response to these two scenarios will tell you more than any amount of thinking ever could. The scenario that creates more openness, more ease, more expansion in your body - that's your answer. It's that simple.

Step 3: Act on What You Get

This is the part where most people get stuck. You get the intuitive hit. You know what feels right. But then you second-guess it. You start thinking about all the reasons it might not work. You wonder if you're just making it up. You ask three friends for their opinion, and now you're more confused than when you started.

The only way to build trust with your intuition is to act on it. Even if it's scary. Even if it doesn't make logical sense. Even if other people think you're crazy. You have to take the step.

And here's the thing: you don't have to take a big step. You can take a small one. If your intuition is telling you to explore a particular spiritual path, you don't have to quit your job and join an ashram. You can just read one book about it. You can attend one workshop. You can try one practice and see how it feels.

Small actions build trust. Each time you act on your intuition and it leads you somewhere good, you strengthen the connection. Each time you ignore it and end up in a dead end, you weaken it. The more you act, the clearer the signal gets.

What to Do When Your Intuition Feels Silent

Sometimes your intuition goes quiet. You sit down to check in, and you get nothing. No feeling, no sensation, no knowing. Just blankness. This is normal. It doesn't mean you're doing it wrong. It doesn't mean you don't have intuition. It just means there's some interference.

Interference can come from a lot of places. Stress, exhaustion, emotional overwhelm, caffeine, alcohol, too much screen time, too much social interaction, not enough sleep, not enough alone time. Your nervous system needs to be in a certain state for your intuition to come through clearly. If you're running on empty, the signal gets scrambled.

When this happens, don't force it. Don't try harder. Instead, take care of your nervous system. Get some sleep. Take a walk in nature. Take a break from your phone. Spend some time alone. Do something that calms you down and brings you back to yourself.

Your intuition is like a radio station. It's always broadcasting. But if your receiver is full of static, you can't hear it. Clearing the static is not about doing more - it's about doing less. It's about creating conditions for the signal to come through naturally.

If you're looking for a practical tool to help you tune in more consistently, I've found that working with a journal designed specifically for intuitive decision-making can be incredibly helpful. See on Amazon (paid link) - this one has prompts that guide you through exactly the kind of body-based checking I'm talking about.

The Difference Between Intuition and Fear

One of the most common questions I get is: "How do I know if it's intuition or just fear?" It's a good question, because fear can feel a lot like intuition. Both of them create strong physical sensations. Both of them can make you feel like you know something for sure.

Here's the key difference: fear contracts, and intuition expands. Fear feels tight, urgent, and reactive. It pushes you away from something. It makes you want to close down, protect yourself, and retreat. Intuition, on the other hand, feels open, calm, and clear. It pulls you toward something. It makes you want to open up, engage, and move forward.

Fear says "no" from a place of survival. Intuition says "no" from a place of wisdom. And sometimes they look the same on the surface, but they feel completely different in the body.

If you're not sure which one you're dealing with, try this: instead of focusing on the decision, focus on the feeling itself. Breathe into it. Ask it what it wants you to know. If it's fear, it will soften when you give it attention. If it's intuition, it will clarify and strengthen.

Another way to tell: fear is usually about the future. It's worried about what might happen. Intuition is about the present. It's showing you what's true right now. If your feeling is full of "what ifs" and worst-case scenarios, it's probably fear. If it's a quiet, grounded knowing that doesn't need to explain itself, it's probably intuition.

Why Your Spiritual Path Will Keep Changing

Here's something nobody tells you about spiritual paths: they're not permanent. You don't find your path and then stay on it forever. Your path changes as you change. What worked for you five years ago might not work for you now. What feels true today might feel false tomorrow.

This is not a sign that you're doing it wrong. It's a sign that you're growing. Your spiritual path is not a destination - it's a living, breathing relationship with the mystery. And like any relationship, it evolves over time.

Your intuition will guide you through these changes if you let it. It will tell you when it's time to move on. It will tell you when it's time to go deeper. It will tell you when something that used to feel alive now feels dead. All you have to do is listen.

If you're struggling with the idea of changing paths because you feel like you've invested too much time or energy in your current one, that's not your intuition talking - that's your ego. Your ego wants consistency. Your ego wants to be right about the choices you've made. Your intuition doesn't care about any of that. It only cares about what's true for you right now.

Letting go of a spiritual path that no longer serves you is not failure. It's growth. And your intuition will guide you through it if you trust it.

A Practical Tool for Getting Unstuck

Sometimes you're just stuck. You've checked in with your body, you've cleared the static, you've ruled out fear, and you still don't know what to do. This happens. It's not a problem. It's just a sign that you need a different approach.

When I get stuck, I use a technique called "The Two Doors." I imagine two doors in front of me. One door represents option A, and the other represents option B. I walk up to the first door and I stand in front of it. I notice what my body feels like when I'm standing at this door. Then I walk to the second door and do the same thing. The door that feels more open, more spacious, more inviting - that's my answer.

If neither door feels right, that's also information. It means neither option is aligned. It means I need to wait, or look for a third option that I haven't considered yet.

Another tool I love is working with a deck of cards or an oracle system that's designed to bypass the analytical mind and speak directly to the intuition. See on Amazon (paid link) - this one is excellent for getting quick, clear answers when you're feeling foggy.

The Only Rule: Trust Your Body More Than Your Thoughts

when it comes down to it, using your intuition to make decisions about your spiritual path comes down to one simple rule: trust your body more than your thoughts.

Your thoughts will tell you a thousand different things. They'll tell you that you need more information, that you should wait, that you might be wrong, that you should ask someone else, that you're not qualified to decide. Your thoughts will keep you stuck in a loop forever if you let them.

Your body, on the other hand, will tell you one thing clearly. It will give you a yes or a no. It will show you what's aligned and what's not. It will guide you home.

The more you practice this, the easier it gets. The more you trust your body, the louder your intuition becomes. The more you act on what you receive, the clearer the signal gets. It's a feedback loop that builds on itself.

And here's the beautiful thing: you don't have to be perfect at this. You don't have to get it right every time. You're allowed to make mistakes. You're allowed to change your mind. You're allowed to try something and discover it's not for you. That's not failure - that's learning. That's how you build the relationship with your intuition.

If you're looking for a complete guide to developing this skill from the ground up, I highly recommend this book. See on Amazon (paid link) - it walks you through exactly how
